subtopic_detail: Eczema herpeticum recognition in a child with atopic dermatitis
stem: A 4-year-old girl with moderate atopic dermatitis presents with a 36 hour history of a worsening rash on her face and neck, fevers, and pain on touching the lesions. Examination shows clustered punched-out monomorphic erosions with yellow crusting overlying eczematous skin on her cheeks, neck, and upper trunk. She has not used new topical products and is currently on mometasone furoate 0.1% ointment for flares.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 28, T 38.6, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Clustered punched-out monomorphic erosions with crust on cheeks, neck, upper trunk; eczematous background; cervical lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Skin swab for viral PCR taken at triage

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Admit for intravenous aciclovir with paediatric infectious diseases and ophthalmology involvement
options.rationale: Eczema herpeticum is a dermatological emergency requiring intravenous aciclovir, with ophthalmology review if any periocular involvement to prevent corneal disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema herpeticum)

options.text: Up-titrate topical mometasone furoate to twice daily across all involved sites for 7 days
options.rationale: Potent topical steroids alone do not treat herpes simplex infection and may worsen disseminated viral disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Atopic eczema)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg four times daily for impetiginised eczema as outpatient management
options.rationale: Impetigo gives honey crust but lacks the monomorphic punched-out vesicular erosions of eczema herpeticum and would not be treated alone. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: skin and soft tissue)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: skin and soft tissue

options.text: Wet wrap therapy with diluted topical steroid alone and review at the eczema clinic in 1 week
options.rationale: Wet wraps over active herpes simplex virus may spread infection and delay needed antiviral therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Atopic eczema)

options.text: Oral valaciclovir 20 mg/kg three times daily for 7 days and discharge home with safety netting
options.rationale: Severe eczema herpeticum with systemic features or facial involvement warrants intravenous aciclovir not oral therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema herpeticum)

explanation.summary: Eczema herpeticum is herpes simplex virus infection complicating atopic dermatitis, presenting with clustered monomorphic punched-out vesiculo-erosions, often with fever and lymphadenopathy. It is a dermatological emergency in children because of the risk of disseminated viral disease, bacterial superinfection, and keratoconjunctivitis. Intravenous aciclovir is started promptly while viral PCR confirms the diagnosis, with ophthalmology review for periocular disease. Bacterial superinfection is treated with appropriate antibiotics and topical steroids resume only once the viral infection is controlled.
key_points: Monomorphic punched-out erosions with fever in an eczematous child point to eczema herpeticum
Intravenous aciclovir, ophthalmology, and infectious diseases input are warranted for facial or severe disease
Topical steroids alone or wet wraps over active disease can worsen viral spread
explanation.pearls: Periocular eczema herpeticum requires same-day ophthalmology review because herpes simplex keratitis can cause permanent corneal scarring and vision loss.

sources.label: eTG Antibiotic: skin and soft tissue

subtopic_detail: Iron deficiency anaemia in a toddler with excess cow milk intake
stem: A 22-month-old boy is referred by his maternal child health nurse for pallor and decreased activity over the past 2 months. He drinks approximately 1 litre of full cream cow milk daily and is a fussy eater. Family is Australian-born without known haemoglobinopathy. He has tongue pallor and a soft systolic flow murmur. His parents are otherwise reassured by his behaviour and growth which is at the 25th centile for weight and 50th centile for height.

data_table.Examination: Pallor of tongue and conjunctivae, soft systolic flow murmur at left sternal edge, no organomegaly, no purpura
data_table.Investigations: Hb 84 g/L, MCV 64 fL, MCH 19 pg, ferritin 8 micrograms/L, CRP 3 mg/L, WCC and platelets normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Intravenous iron carboxymaltose infusion today as first-line because oral iron is poorly tolerated
options.rationale: Intravenous iron is reserved for malabsorption, intolerance of oral iron, or severe symptomatic anaemia not responding to oral therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Oral elemental iron 3 to 6 mg/kg daily for 3 months and reduce cow milk to under 500 mL daily
options.rationale: Iron deficiency anaemia in toddlers is treated with oral iron with dietary advice to limit cow milk and increase iron-rich foods. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Packed red cell transfusion of 10 mL/kg over 4 hours and admit for further evaluation
options.rationale: Transfusion is reserved for symptomatic anaemia with cardiovascular compromise; this child has stable haemodynamics. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Haemoglobin electrophoresis and bone marrow biopsy now to exclude thalassaemia and aplasia
options.rationale: Microcytosis with low ferritin in a milk-drinking toddler is iron deficiency until proven otherwise; further workup waits on therapeutic response. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Increased dietary iron-rich foods alone with repeat full blood count in 3 months
options.rationale: Established anaemia with ferritin 8 micrograms/L will not respond to diet alone in clinically meaningful time. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency anaemia in early childhood is a common nutritional disorder in Australia, particularly in milk-drinking toddlers and those with restricted diets. Diagnostic criteria are microcytic hypochromic anaemia with low ferritin (below 12 micrograms/L) in the absence of inflammation. Treatment is oral elemental iron 3 to 6 mg/kg daily for 3 months with dietary modification to limit cow milk to under 500 mL daily and introduce iron-rich foods. Haemoglobin should rise by 10 g/L within 4 weeks; failure of response prompts evaluation for malabsorption, ongoing losses, or alternative diagnoses.
key_points: Ferritin under 12 micrograms/L without inflammation confirms iron deficiency in children
Oral elemental iron 3 to 6 mg/kg daily for 3 months is first-line in stable iron deficiency anaemia
Cow milk over 500 mL daily after 12 months impairs iron status and is a key target for dietary advice
explanation.pearls: Repeat haemoglobin at 4 weeks of oral iron should rise by at least 10 g/L; a flatter response prompts review for adherence, ongoing blood loss, or malabsorption such as coeliac disease.

sources.label: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood: paediatric iron deficiency

subtopic_detail: Incomplete Kawasaki disease beyond day 10 with persistent fever
stem: A 3-year-old boy is admitted on day 11 of illness with persistent fever to 39.5 degrees Celsius despite a 5 day course of empirical amoxicillin. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, cracked lips, a polymorphous truncal rash, and unilateral cervical lymphadenopathy of 2.5 cm. There is no extremity desquamation yet. Echocardiography shows a left anterior descending coronary artery z-score of 2.6.

data_table.Examination: Bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection without exudate, cracked fissured lips, polymorphous trunk rash, 2.5 cm right cervical node, no rash desquamation
data_table.Investigations: CRP 168 mg/L, ESR 88 mm/h, ALT 78 U/L, platelets 612 x10^9/L, Hb 96 g/L, albumin 27 g/L, urine WCC raised, blood cultures negative; echo LAD z-score 2.6

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg single infusion plus oral aspirin 3 to 5 mg/kg daily
options.rationale: AU paediatric practice treats Kawasaki disease, including late-presenting and incomplete cases with coronary changes, with IVIG and antiplatelet aspirin to reduce coronary risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg daily for 3 days as primary monotherapy
options.rationale: Steroids are an adjunct in primary refractory or high-risk disease, not the sole initial treatment in Australian practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Empirical intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg six hourly for staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
options.rationale: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome causes flaccid bullae and skin tenderness, not non-purulent conjunctivitis and coronary changes. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: skin and soft tissue)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: skin and soft tissue

options.text: Subcutaneous infliximab loading dose 5 mg/kg today as primary refractory disease treatment
options.rationale: TNF-alpha blockers are reserved for IVIG-refractory disease as second or third line therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Symptomatic care with paracetamol and ibuprofen with echocardiography review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Delayed treatment of Kawasaki disease beyond day 10 still benefits from IVIG when coronary or inflammatory markers persist. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease can be diagnosed when fewer than 4 of the principal clinical features are present alongside fever for at least 5 days, supported by laboratory criteria (elevated CRP/ESR, anaemia, hypoalbuminaemia, raised ALT, sterile pyuria, thrombocytosis after day 7) or coronary changes on echocardiography. Treatment with intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg and aspirin remains indicated even beyond day 10 when fever persists or coronary changes are evident. High-risk features (young age, low albumin, elevated CRP, hyponatraemia, coronary involvement) may prompt addition of corticosteroids or infliximab in tertiary centres. Echocardiography is repeated at 2 and 6 weeks.
key_points: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is diagnosed by laboratory criteria or coronary abnormalities supporting prolonged fever with under 4 principal features
IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin is the standard treatment, even after day 10 when inflammation or coronary changes persist
Adjunct steroids or infliximab apply to high-risk or refractory disease
explanation.pearls: A coronary artery z-score of 2.5 or greater on echocardiography is enough to confirm incomplete Kawasaki disease and mandate IVIG, regardless of how many classical criteria are met.

sources.label: American Heart Association Kawasaki disease scientific statement
eTG Antibiotic: skin and soft tissue

subtopic_detail: IgA vasculitis with proteinuria: renal involvement assessment
stem: A 7-year-old girl presents with palpable purpura distributed over her buttocks and lower legs, with bilateral ankle swelling and intermittent colicky abdominal pain over the past 5 days. The rash followed a viral upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago. She has not had macroscopic haematuria. Her parents ask about the next step in monitoring.

data_table.Examination: Palpable purpura over buttocks and extensor surfaces of lower legs, bilateral ankle synovitis, soft non-tender abdomen, no scrotal swelling, no neurology
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis dipstick blood 2+ protein 3+; urine protein creatinine ratio 280 mg/mmol; creatinine 52 micromol/L; albumin 32 g/L; platelets 320 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg daily for 3 days followed by oral mycophenolate mofetil
options.rationale: Pulsed steroids and mycophenolate apply to crescentic nephritis after biopsy confirmation, not first-line outpatient escalation. (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases Guideline)

options.text: Discharge with oral analgesia and review with the general practitioner in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Significant proteinuria is the dominant determinant of long-term renal outcome and requires specialist assessment, not routine general practitioner review. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Empirical oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg four times daily for 7 days as post-streptococcal cover
options.rationale: HSP nephritis is immune complex disease, not infection; routine antibiotics are not indicated. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Admit for paediatric nephrology review and start oral prednisolone 1 to 2 mg/kg daily
options.rationale: Significant proteinuria with HSP (urine PCR over 250 mg/mmol) warrants nephrology referral and consideration of corticosteroid therapy with biopsy for nephrotic-range disease or persistent abnormality. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Renal tract ultrasound today to exclude obstructive uropathy as the cause of proteinuria
options.rationale: HSP nephritis is parenchymal glomerular disease; ultrasound has no role in screening for it in this setting. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch Schonlein purpura)

explanation.summary: IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schonlein purpura) is the commonest childhood vasculitis and presents with palpable purpura, arthritis, abdominal pain, and renal involvement. Long-term outcome is dominated by renal disease; about 1 in 3 children develop nephritis ranging from microscopic haematuria to nephrotic-nephritic syndrome. AU practice screens with serial urinalysis and urine protein:creatinine ratio for 6 months from onset, with paediatric nephrology referral for persistent proteinuria over 100 to 250 mg/mmol, hypertension, deteriorating function, or nephrotic features. Steroids reduce arthralgia and abdominal pain but their role in altering long-term renal outcome remains debated and they are reserved for selected cases by specialists.
key_points: Significant proteinuria (urine PCR over 250 mg/mmol) in HSP prompts nephrology review
Serial urinalysis and BP measurement are continued for 6 months from onset
Steroids are considered for severe abdominal pain or renal involvement rather than routinely
explanation.pearls: Renal biopsy is considered for HSP children with persistent nephrotic syndrome, deteriorating renal function, or persistent proteinuria over 100 mg/mmol beyond 4 to 6 weeks of conservative management.

subtopic_detail: Bronchiolitis failing high flow nasal cannula: escalation decision
stem: A 4-month-old former 38 week infant has been admitted with RSV bronchiolitis. She has been on humidified high flow nasal cannula oxygen 2 L/kg/min at FiO2 0.5 for the past 4 hours. She remains tachypnoeic with marked subcostal recessions, intermittent grunting, and rising work of breathing. Capillary blood gas shows pH 7.22, pCO2 70 mmHg, HCO3 25 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L. She is becoming less responsive.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 78 with intermittent apnoeas, SpO2 88% on HFNC FiO2 0.5, T 38.0
data_table.Examination: Marked subcostal and intercostal recession, intermittent grunting, head bobbing, drowsy, reduced air entry bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: Capillary gas pH 7.22 pCO2 70 mmHg HCO3 25 mmol/L lactate 1.4 mmol/L; CXR hyperinflation without focal consolidation; RSV PCR positive

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Escalate to paediatric intensive care for non-invasive ventilation and intubation readiness
options.rationale: Rising CO2, respiratory acidosis, exhaustion, and apnoea on maximal HFNC are indications for ICU escalation with CPAP or intubation in infant bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial nebulised hypertonic saline 3% every 4 hours to improve mucus clearance and oxygenation
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is not recommended for routine acute care in bronchiolitis and does not reverse impending respiratory failure. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg and inhaled salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer hourly for 3 hours
options.rationale: Salbutamol and corticosteroids are not effective in viral bronchiolitis in infants under 12 months. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Empirical intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily for secondary bacterial pneumonia coverage
options.rationale: Antibiotics are not routinely indicated in bronchiolitis without a focal consolidation or strong evidence of bacterial superinfection. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Continue current HFNC settings and observe for another 4 hours before considering escalation
options.rationale: Continued observation in the face of rising CO2 and exhaustion delays definitive ventilatory support and risks decompensation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is the leading cause of infant hospital admission in Australia and is managed with supportive care including nasal suction, hydration, and supplemental oxygen. High-flow nasal cannula is used for moderate disease, with thresholds for escalation that include rising CO2, persistent hypoxia despite maximal HFNC, apnoea, or exhaustion. Continuous positive airway pressure or intubation are delivered in paediatric intensive care for failure to respond to HFNC. Bronchodilators, hypertonic saline, antibiotics, and corticosteroids are not recommended for routine bronchiolitis.
key_points: HFNC failure includes rising CO2, exhaustion, apnoea, or persistent hypoxia at maximum settings
CPAP and intubation are the next steps in paediatric intensive care
Bronchodilators and steroids are not effective in viral bronchiolitis under 12 months
explanation.pearls: An infant on maximal HFNC with rising CO2 above 60 mmHg, falling pH below 7.25, exhaustion, or recurrent apnoea needs ICU escalation rather than further HFNC titration.
